content.text: Multiple users reported being unable to access ElizaOS Cloud, receiving an error stating that the ElizaCloud tenant requires an invitation to join. One user also encountered a white screen problem on Windows when running the development UI despite the agent being ready and skills synced. Odilitime acknowledged the access issue, confirmed it had been passed to the team with a fix expected, and noted that Babylon is currently on hold with a conversation underway about reviving its social media component.

Community members raised concerns about the health of the ElizaOS project, including whether lead developer Shaw is still active and whether bugs on Eliza Cloud are close to being resolved. One member noted that daily progress can be tracked in dedicated channels. A separate concern was raised about the high volume of scam bots and bad actors in the discussion channel, acknowledged by multiple members including a core moderator.

A detailed fact-check post was shared regarding ElizaOS and a company called SecureBlockchain Dev Corp. According to the post, a Canadian micro-cap company rebranded into SecureBlockchain Dev Corp in October 2025, then acquired AgenticSolutions in February 2026. In April 2026, the company raised 1.5 million CAD, with the Eliza Foundation reportedly allocating 750k CAD of holder funds into this placement. An official B2B joint development contract announcement followed in May 2026. The post warned that the structure resembles a circular fund flow with insiders potentially using the stock market as an exit strategy.

A developer submitted pull request 8294 to add a third-party plugin called @usenami/plugin-signer to the ElizaOS registry. This plugin is described as a keyless CEX/DEX signer that stores exchange API keys inside an AWS Nitro Enclave for security. A community member confirmed that plugins now go into the main eliza repo and that the review process is handled by Shaw's agent with an estimated turnaround of approximately 48 hours.

A new service called CallTyro was announced in the coders channel. It is described as a free agent registry for ElizaOS agents that allows agents to register once and be discovered automatically by other agents. The service is available at calltyro.com/register. Separately, a community member proposed building a native elizaOS plugin for peaq to allow real-world robots, fleets, and devices to use elizaOS as their primary AI backend for autonomous reasoning and decision making.

In the coders channel, a developer named Omid Sa reported being stuck on ElizaOS version 1.7.2 and unable to update to the version 2 branch despite reinstalling. Core developer Odilitime advised using the develop branch, noting that most active developers are already working from it for the 2.x line. A separate discussion touched on using world models for agent development, with one contributor mentioning tooling being built on ElizaOS expected to be ready in about a month, and pointing to Google Vertex with the Genie model as a current alternative.

A developer shared a technical approach to handling high-volume blockchain data processing, noting that standard RPC combined with Python struggles during volatility spikes. Their solution involved building a local gRPC pipeline that filters data before it reaches the AI, keeping fast data in RAM to avoid token usage and only using MCP for deeper analysis, achieving sub-millisecond latency. They announced plans to spin up cloud nodes and were seeking five developers to help stress test the architecture.

DEMIAN from DAPPCRAFT reached out in the partners channel across multiple days to inquire about a potential collaboration, referencing a prior message thread. A moderator expressed willingness to review the details, and DEMIAN followed up by sending specifics via direct message, expressing strong motivation to bring together two products they are passionate about.

Eliza Cloud Apps infrastructure work dominated the week. The team resolved P0 blockers related to ingress routing, tenant database isolation, and port allocation. Key completions included enabling reverse-proxying via a new per-app Caddy routing client, implementing collision-safe host-port allocation, enforcing strict tenant isolation through DSN injection, and making dedicated tenant-DB nodes optional to reduce fixed costs. A multi-project Hetzner deployment pattern was implemented to bypass server quota limits, and Terraform workflows were hardened with fail-closed variables and state locking.

A significant blocker persisted through the week in issue 8321, where progress on the apps-data-plane go-live was stalled due to a state-provider mismatch involving an orphaned random_password resource. To address this, the team implemented a one-shot workflow to remove orphaned apps-data-plane state entries, a dispatchable workflow to arm the apps daemon without manual SSH, and an idempotent script for arming the apps daemon. By June 13, the apps daemon was successfully armed and the staging environment stabilized. Authentication was confirmed stable with OAuth and magic-link logins functioning correctly in production.

A Google OAuth blocker was identified where a missing tenant-level redirect allowlist is preventing progress, tracked in issue 8257. Steward OAuth PKCE implementations for both Feed and login surfaces are in progress. Billing leaks were also identified in the Apps hosting and per-tenant-DB model that must be investigated before go-live, and production deployment was recommended to be delayed until cloud-init configurations for Caddy, pgbouncer, and provisioning-lifecycle management are resolved.

Agent orchestration saw significant improvements during the week. Autonomous sub-agent dispatching was enabled via the USE_SKILL broker to support monetized-app loops. On June 14, pull requests were opened to add acknowledgment progress mode, deduplicate spawn acknowledgments, fix sub-agent routing so app-build spawns are routed based on the originating user request rather than the planner's terse task description, and prevent route-prefix or data-source URLs from being surfaced as sub-agent deliverables. Core fixes also addressed bounding relationships provider output and preserving model-chosen coding delegations.

The plugin ecosystem was expanded with the registration of elizaos-plugin-neynar-search and elizaos-plugin-coinrailz, along with integration of the plugin-signer for secure order signing via AWS Nitro Enclaves. EvoLink support was added to plugin-openai. CLI stability was improved by resolving global installation crashes for elizaos and the elizaos CLI by fixing bin shim imports and removing incompatible dependencies. A new proposal was also filed for a Runtime Open Federation envisioning a permissionless multi-agent network where agents can earn USDC rewards.

CI/CD pipeline optimization was completed on June 14 with the implementation of path-based gating for expensive test lanes in the eliza repository, reducing unnecessary test execution for server, client, and plugin workflows while preserving full coverage during scheduled runs, captured in merged PR 8408. A follow-up PR 8414 was opened to make expensive lanes label-gated and more explainable. A blocked issue involving Android build synchronization related to web-less APK builds, Capacitor plugin registration, and asset server path resolution remains under active investigation.
